meta.title: Kompass: Ensuring SPL-Token Stays Safe on Pinocchio

transcript: Hello everyone. My name is Daniel Cumming and I'm here from Runtime Verification to talk to you about Kompass, which we're using to prove the equivalence of the Solana Token Program with Pinocchio as the backend. I got a lot to get through so I'm going to probably talk fast so buckle up everyone. The Solana Token Program is the topic of our talk. You might know that at the moment this is using SPL-Token as its backend but shortly it will be upgraded to using P-Token. The difference between the two is that the SPL-Token is using the Solana SDK whereas the P-Token is using the Pinocchio library. The benefits of which is that Pinocchio is more efficient and so P-Token is going to be more efficient. But while we want the change to be an increase in efficiency, we do not want there to be a change in behavior. We still want to have the Solana Token Program that we have come to know and love. So we are in a reality where there's two programs which are essentially trying to do the same thing but they are slightly different. We do want there to be a difference in efficiency. So the intersection of which is the idea of what ought to be the Solana Token Program. So we can be explicit and talk about what this is in particular. And what we want to be the same is all of the logic of the program should be as we expect, the minting, the transferring, the burning, the effects of which should be the same if we do the upgrade to P-Token. And we want to know that the errors are happening under the same conditions and that we get the same error code. We don't want there to be any new behaviors or any new restrictions when we perform the update. And so that leaves what we want to be different is simply that it's more efficient. That's what we want, lower CUs. How are we going to check this? Well, in determining equivalence between two programs we have a few different options. In increasing order of confidence, the first thing that we might do is a manual review. So an expert would look at both programs and try and determine that they are in fact doing the same thing. This was performed by Neodyme and Zellic. They did a fantastic job. Kudos to them. And we would still want more confidence that the programs are going to behave the same way so we might do differential testing. This is where we have a test suite, we run both programs through the same test suite and we check, do we get the same results for those inputs. The fuzzing for for this was done by Neodyme as part of the engagement that I spoke of earlier. And we also are running the integration and Mollusk test suites as we write our specifications. We're writing specifications because the next highest confidence I guess that we can get is formal verification that the two programs are equivalent. So using the power of mathematics we can prove that they're going to do the same thing. This is the approach that we're taking and I'll describe to you what one of these proofs is. So a formal proof of code correctness, I have a diagram here to explain what that is. In the middle we have the proof engine. This is the, this is what Kompass would do in this case. And it can output uh some uh we can be proved correct or it can be proved incorrect. It needs some input which is on the the left hand side and there I have color coded yellow is the implementation. This is the code that Anza wrote which is uh what we would actually have executing when we perform a transaction. And uh the specification is split into a pre-condition and a post-condition. The pre-condition is what is the generalized valid initial state that must hold in order for us to call the implementation. And the post-condition is the guarantee of what must hold if we do call that implementation and we satisfy the pre-condition. So this is where we we know what the effects ought to be of the program and we check that that did in fact or that will in fact happen for all inputs. So bringing this to P-Token, uh what we have for the implementation in the yellow there is the P-Token program. This is written in Rust by Anza. And then the specification, the pre-condition and the post-condition, this is written by Runtime Verification. This is where we assume the valid initial state and we assert the valid final state. All of this gets you know bundled up and put into the Kompass prover which is the the step in the middle, the proof engine. Uh this is written in our K Framework. And from there we can prove that the implementation is satisfying the specification. In which case we're happy, we can go party. If it fails we might cry, we'll we'll find out. But we're going to do the partying, it's going to work. Uh that's not enough though. We also need to uh do this for SPL-Token. So we need to do the same proof again. Now the implementation, the yellow box there has changed to have SPL-Token program. And we use the same specification. So by proving both we end up in this uh well yeah the same specification is going to prove both implementations. So we do two proofs and then we use the cross product of the proofs which is could be formatted slightly better I think but anyway you get the idea. We want both proofs to pass. And and what that is communicating is that both SPL-Token and P-Token are are in that intersection that we spoke about where they are we have proved that they are correct implementations for what the Solana program ought to do. And by nature of them both being correct implementations we can assert that they are equivalent modulo that specification. So that's our definition of correctness. To give you some idea of what this looks like in particular, on the left hand side uh this is a proof harness that we would prove an instruction's behavior correct with. So using the same color coding, the blue box up the top, that is the pre-condition. This is where we're assuming the valid initial state. And the yellow box, this is the instruction that Anza wrote that is actually going to process MintTo. So this is for the example MintTo. And then afterwards we need to assert did we error under the conditions that we expected, did we get the correct error code. And also if we didn't error and we're in the success case, did the supply get increased, did the destination account receive their tokens. On the right hand side is a graphical representation of what this proof sort of looks like. Of course it it ends up actually being in some machine language but uh we can think of it as it's an exploration of all possible states. To give a little bit more insight into what's going on, the the harness on the left hand side, what we're doing in particular is we're setting up the the inputs of the particular uh instruction symbolically. So MintTo takes some account infos and some instruction data. When you're performing a test you will instantiate these with concrete values and and you will run that test and you will get some information about those specific values that you tested. We want to represent these values symbolically so we can express all paths and have a higher degree of confidence. That's why in the Pinocchio account there we have question mark borrow for the borrow state, is it a signer, is it writable, is it executable. We need to think of the same idea of the cross product, what are all the options together. Same thing for the input data, we want to look at all of the bytes symbolically. From there we also know that in order to mint we need a we need a mint and we need a destination account to receive the tokens. We also represent these uh the mint and the destination account symbolically. So the authority for the mint, the supply, the decimals, whether or not it's initialized, the freeze authority, all of these are represented symbolically. Once we've established our initial symbolic state we can start traversing a graph and looking at all of the potential. So one thing that we might start with is is it true or is it false that the token mint is initialized or uninitialized. We need to look at both branches and so straight away we have a branching condition. And then the next thing we might look at is is the destination account within the range of less than or equal to. And so this is saying the only valid states that we would expect is uh initialized, uninitialized or frozen but we also need to consider that we're in an invalid state. And so you can see that if you do this for everything that might change you get an a massive tree of everything that could possibly happen. Once you go down to the end along your branches we want to be able to look at the path condition and decide what ought on this branch the result be for this to be a correct implementation of the Solana Token Program. And so this is where we assert are we getting the error code that we expect or are we getting the state update that we expect. All of that needs to be done for each instruction and also sometimes the instructions behave differently under different conditions. And so it's a repeated process that needs to be done across 27 instruction proofs and 20 multisig proofs. But then we multiply that by two because we got P-Token and SPL-Token so there ends up being quite a lot of proofs that need to be done. And in order to do them we need to write out a sound semantics of Rust so that the the Kompass prover is able to do these proofs. So this was a lot of work and originally for the first cut these proofs were running on the order of over five hours each. So this is not a great feedback time to get for anything in engineering. We've reduced that down to about 10 to 20 minutes a run for most of these proofs and we expect that we're going to get them more efficient shortly. Uh that's everything that I have for you today. I thought I'd give you a flavor of what these proofs are doing. Thank you very much for your time.

tags.impact_reason: This is a technical deep dive into the formal verification process for the upcoming upgrade of the Solana Token Program to the more efficient Pinocchio library, ensuring critical network security.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a futuristic verification instrument meticulously scanning two geometrically identical, yet materially different, crystalline structures as the 'Semantic Anchor'. This directly visualizes the core story: the formal, mathematical proof of equivalence between the old SPL-Token and the new, more efficient P-Token. The crystals represent the complex programs, and the instrument represents the Kompass verification process. I included the diegetic text '0%' as a holographic readout from the device to powerfully convey the primary goal and outcome of the migration: 'zero behavioral changes'.
visual_idea.description: A macro shot focusing on the point of contact between a scanning laser and one of the crystalline objects. The intense beam of light penetrates the crystal's surface, refracting through its complex internal geometric lattice. We see the hyper-detailed texture of the brushed metal clamp holding the crystal in place, emphasizing the precision of the verification process. The image captures the immense complexity of the program being analyzed at a microscopic level. NO TEXT.

The intense, volumetric beam of light pierces the crystal's surface, dramatically refracting and scattering through its intricate internal geometric lattice, revealing a hidden, immense complexity. The frame is filled with the hyper-detailed, finely brushed texture of the metallic clamp securely holding the crystal, emphasizing the extreme precision and advanced nature of the scientific verification process. Subtle reflections play across the brushed metal, indicating polished surfaces and engineered design. The scene conveys the microscopic analysis of a complex program. 1:1 aspect ratio, 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), volumetric lighting, global illumination, shallow depth of field, micro-details, ultra-photorealistic, high dynamic range, studio render.

editorial.headline: Runtime Verification Formally Proves P-Token Safety for Anza
editorial.standfirst: The critical migration of the Solana Token Program to the Pinocchio runtime is undergoing mathematical formal verification to guarantee zero behavioral changes. This ensures the ecosystem gains execution efficiency without risking the stability of billions in on-chain assets.
key_takeaways: Runtime Verification is using Kompass to prove mathematical equivalence between the legacy SPL-Token and the new P-Token backend.
The P-Token implementation was authored by Anza, with preliminary manual audits and fuzzing completed by Neodyme and Zellic.
The verification scope includes 27 instruction proofs and 20 multisig proofs, with symbolic execution times optimized to under 20 minutes.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The shift from the legacy SPL-Token program to the **Pinocchio**-based P-Token represents a massive infrastructure upgrade for Solana, aiming to drastically reduce Compute Units (CUs) and enhance validator throughput. However, efficiency cannot come at the cost of stability. **Runtime Verification** has deployed its **Kompass** engine to mathematically prove that P-Token behaves exactly like its predecessor, ensuring that the bedrock of Solana DeFi remains immutable during this transition.

Unlike standard security audits, this process involves formal verification—the highest standard of cryptographic security. By creating a shared specification for both the legacy **Solana SDK** implementation and **Anza**'s new Rust-based P-Token, the team proves equivalence modulo that specification. This goes beyond the manual reviews and fuzzing already completed by **Neodyme** and **Zellic**, offering a mathematical guarantee that minting, burning, and error codes remain identical across both runtimes regardless of the underlying library changes.

The verification effort is exhaustive, covering 27 distinct instruction proofs and 20 multisig proofs. By utilizing symbolic execution to explore every possible state path—rather than just concrete inputs—**Kompass** ensures no edge case is left unchecked. With proof runtimes optimized from five hours down to roughly 10 minutes, this establishes a continuous verification pipeline that allows **Anza** to ship optimizations without fear of introducing regression bugs into the protocol’s most critical primitive.
